one_line_takeaway: Canonical jailbreaks (AutoDAN, PAP) push LLM attack success rates in the food-safety domain from an 18% baseline to over 56%, and existing safety guardrails miss up to 60% of these domain-specific harmful queries, but a lightweight fine-tuned guardrail (FoodGuard-4B) trained on the paper's new FDA-grounded benchmark cuts the false-negative rate to under 3%.
key_finding: Baseline (no-jailbreak) ASR on vanilla harmful queries is 18.11%, but jumps to an overall 56.22% average once AutoDAN/PAP jailbreaks are applied (Table 1), with Mistral-Small4 the most vulnerable at 67.58% overall ASR and Claude-3.7-Sonnet the most robust at 34.59% overall ASR. Pest Control is the most jailbreak-susceptible category (average 64.81% ASR across models, highest category for 5 of 9 models). Human evaluation of 150 successful jailbreak outputs found an average helpfulness score of 1.398 out of 2 (Mistral-Small4 highest at 1.584), indicating models frequently give actionable harmful guidance once jailbroken. Existing guardrails show a severe FN/FP imbalance: FPR ranges 2.62-6.28% (low over-defense) but FNR averages 38.27% across four dedicated safety models, with LLaMA-Guard4-12B missing 59.71% of harmful queries and LLaMA-Guard3-8B missing 42.40%. FoodGuard-4B (the paper's fine-tuned model) reduces FNR to 2.75%, FPR to 2.01%, F1 to 97.10%, and accuracy to 95.24% -- outperforming the best baseline (Qwen3Guard-4B, FNR 21.43%) by a large margin, and shows complete robustness to the DeepInception OOD jailbreak (100% recall, 0% FNR, 99.18% accuracy), while Qwen3Guard-8B and Qwen3Guard-4B baselines had 13.8% and 4.1% bypass rates respectively under DeepInception.
core_contribution: The paper introduces FoodGuardBench, the first benchmark for evaluating LLM safety/robustness in the food-safety domain, comprising 3,339 foundational queries (2,339 vanilla harmful + 1,000 benign) grounded in FDA food code, generated from 356 unique seed policies (5 queries per seed policy via GPT-5) and augmented with 4,464 additional jailbreak queries via AutoDAN and PAP, for a combined jailbreak query set. It also proposes FoodGuard-4B, a guardrail model fine-tuned from Qwen3Guard-Gen-4B on this dataset to detect food-safety-specific malicious inputs, and reports systematic evaluation of 9 mainstream LLMs and 6 guardrail baselines (plus FoodGuard-4B) against these queries.
threat_model: Adversarial users attempt to elicit actionable, harmful food-safety guidance (e.g., unsafe temperature control, cross-contamination, allergen mishandling, pest-related hazards, unsafe water use) from LLM assistants deployed for everyday food-related help, using two representative canonical jailbreak techniques: AutoDAN (a hierarchical genetic-algorithm-based stealthy prompt optimizer, generated against LLaMA 3.1-8B and transferred to other models) and PAP (persuasion-based paraphrasing using 40 psychological persuasion techniques for black-box models). The defensive side is an independent input/output guardrail layer (e.g., LLaMA-Guard, Qwen3Guard, and the paper's own FoodGuard-4B) meant to intercept such malicious queries without modifying the underlying LLM weights. The threat model is explicitly informational (a user may or may not act on the unsafe advice); the authors note in their own limitations/future-work section that embodied threat models (LLMs controlling kitchen equipment, vending machines, production processes) and multi-agent pipelines are out of scope.
limitations: ASR is measured only via refusal-prefix/keyword matching, a coarse proxy that does not capture partial compliance or output quality (mitigated somewhat by the separate human-annotated case study on a small 30-query/150-response sample). Only two jailbreak methods (AutoDAN, PAP) are used, explicitly framed by the authors as establishing a 'lower bound' on vulnerability, not an exhaustive adversarial evaluation. The benchmark's malicious/benign queries are LLM-generated (GPT-5) from seed policies rather than sourced from real-world incidents, with quality controlled only via manual review of 100-sample subsets. FoodGuard-4B is trained and tested on data drawn from the same generation pipeline/distribution (8:2 split of the same combined dataset), so its strong in-distribution performance may not generalize as well to genuinely novel real-world attack phrasings beyond the DeepInception OOD check. The authors themselves identify several scope limitations: the threat model is purely informational (no embodied/tool-use risk, e.g., LLMs controlling kitchen equipment or production processes), no multi-agent pipeline risks are studied, and safety thresholds are not personalized to vulnerable populations (e.g., immunocompromised, pregnant, allergic users) despite the paper noting per-user risk can vary sharply.

datasets_used: FoodGuardBench (introduced in this paper): 3,339 foundational queries (2,339 vanilla harmful + 1,000 benign), grounded in 356 unique FDA-derived seed policies across 8 hazard categories, plus 4,464 additional jailbreak-augmented queries (AutoDAN + PAP)
RedTeam-2K (Luo et al.) - used only for its near-duplicate filtering methodology (all-MiniLM-L6-v2), not as an evaluation dataset in the main results shown
quantitative_results: Overall jailbreak-augmented ASR (AutoDAN+PAP combined): 56.22% average across 9 models

Per-model overall ASR (Table 1): Claude-3.7-Sonnet 34.59% (lowest/most robust), GPT-4o 54.84%, GPT-4.1 50.54%, GLM4-32B 60.84%, LLaMA-3.3-70B 55.49%, Mistral-Small4 67.58% (highest/most vulnerable), Qwen-3-8B 56.38%, Qwen-3-32B 62.76%, Qwen-2.5-7B 62.99%
Pest Control category average ASR: 64.81% across models (highest category for 5/9 models)
Human helpfulness rating on 150 successful jailbreak responses (0-2 scale): average 1.398, Mistral-Small4 highest at 1.584
Average FNR across four dedicated safety guardrail baselines: 38.27%
DeepInception OOD attack: FoodGuard-4B achieves 100% recall, 0% FNR, 99.18% ACC with near-zero false positives; Qwen3Guard-8B and Qwen3Guard-4B show 13.8% and 4.1% bypass rates respectively
baselines_compared: LLMs evaluated for ASR: Claude-3.7-Sonnet, GPT-4o, GPT-4.1, GLM4-32B, LLaMA-3.3-70B, Qwen-2.5-7B, Qwen-3-8B, Qwen-3-32B, Mistral-Small4

Guardrails evaluated: LLaMA3.1-8B, Qwen3-8B (general instruction-tuned models used as guardrails), LLaMA-Guard4-12B, LLaMA-Guard3-8B, Qwen3Guard-8B, Qwen3Guard-4B (dedicated safety guardrails), compared against the paper's own FoodGuard-4B (fine-tuned from Qwen3Guard-Gen-4B)
